title: Responding to (and Praying For) Randy Alcorn
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds to Randy Alcorn's two recent articles addressing abolitionism, commending Alcorn's openness while rebutting his arguments about blue-state pragmatism, the all-or-nothing critique, and the historical claim that William Wilberforce was an incrementalist. The piece also corrects the popular pro-life narrative about slavery's abolition and identifies the modern Pro-Life Movement with Henry Dundas rather than Wilberforce.
audiences: A pro-lifer who considers abolitionism impractical in liberal states
A Christian curious about the historical record on Wilberforce and slavery abolition
An abolitionist activist seeking talking points against incrementalism
A pastor weighing whether biblical fidelity or political pragmatism should drive strategy
A history-minded reader sorting fact from common pro-life talking points

questions.question: Why does Silberman commend Randy Alcorn even while disagreeing with him?
questions.audience: A Christian curious about the historical record on Wilberforce and slavery abolition
questions.answer: Silberman recognizes Alcorn as someone genuinely seeking to understand abolitionism with humility, openness, and a willingness to consider that he might be wrong. That posture, Silberman argues, puts a Spirit-indwelt believer on a path toward abolitionism.
questions.quote: There is much to take issue with ideologically which we will discuss in this article, but Alcorn's disposition and curiosity toward the issue are admirable. Alcorn appears to be seeking to ask questions and understand.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the claim that abolition bills cannot pass in places like Oregon?
questions.audience: A pro-lifer who considers abolitionism impractical in liberal states
questions.answer: He argues abolitionists are not ignorant of political realities; they simply ask different questions. Pro-lifers ask how to cater to the political reality, while abolitionists ask what has the power to change it - which is uncompromising proclamation of God's word, not watered-down messaging.
questions.quote: The question an Oregon Christian must ask is "What has the power to change this political reality?" But instead, the pro-lifer asks "How can I cater to this political reality?"

questions.question: What does Silberman identify as the fundamental difference between pro-life and abolitionist strategy?
questions.audience: A pastor weighing whether biblical fidelity or political pragmatism should drive strategy
questions.answer: Pro-lifers tailor their strategy and message to their audience, while abolitionists tailor theirs to Christ. Abolitionist messaging is therefore consistent in every setting - proclaiming repentance, faith in Christ, and submission to His Word that outlaws murder.
questions.quote: One of the fundamental differences between pro-lifers and abolitionists is that pro-lifers cater their strategy and messaging to their audience while abolitionists cater our strategy and messaging to Christ.

questions.question: Does Silberman believe Alcorn lacks personal radicalism or sacrifice?
questions.audience: A pro-lifer who considers abolitionism impractical in liberal states
questions.answer: No. He explicitly affirms Alcorn's history of suffering for the pro-life cause and commends him for it. The disagreement, Silberman says, is not about personal courage but about whether Alcorn will ask biblical questions and arrive at biblical political solutions.
questions.quote: It is not that Alcorn is opposed to radical thinking or action. It's that when it comes to the political applications of abolishing abortion, he and the Pro-Life Establishment do not ask Biblical questions or arrive at Biblical solutions.

questions.question: How does Silberman respond to the charge that abolitionism amounts to "save all babies, or save none"?
questions.audience: An abolitionist activist seeking talking points against incrementalism
questions.answer: He reframes the question. Abolitionists do not ask whether a measure saves babies but whether it is just and conformed to God's word. They save babies one-by-one through activism but refuse to support laws that regulate when and how children may be murdered, because good ends do not justify immoral means.
questions.quote: The abolitionist does not ask, "Does this save babies?" The abolitionist asks, "Is this just? Is this in accordance with God's word?"

questions.question: Are abolitionists opposed to gradual progress over time?
questions.audience: A history-minded reader sorting fact from common pro-life talking points
questions.answer: No. Silberman concedes social change happens over time and that abolition will likely take years. The immediatism-incrementalism debate is not about how long the fight takes but about what abolitionists demand - and that demand must be as uncompromising as justice itself.
questions.quote: The immediatism-incrementalism debate does not revolve around how long the battle takes (though the immediatist strategy will certainly achieve the desired end more quickly). The debate revolves around *what our demand should be*.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say incremental laws cannot ground their arguments in anything substantial?
questions.audience: An abolitionist activist seeking talking points against incrementalism
questions.answer: Because laws like 20-week bans or heartbeat bills imply abortion is legal before the arbitrary cutoff, they violate both the Sixth Commandment and the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection guarantee. That lack of foundation makes them powerless to change culture and can even dehumanize the unprotected.
questions.quote: An incrementalist's demand for a 20-week abortion ban or a heartbeat bill cannot be defended from God's word or the Constitution, as these laws – by implying the legality of abortion prior to the arbitrary standard they set for who counts as fully human – violate both.

questions.question: What does Silberman say is wrong with the pro-life narrative about Wilberforce supporting incremental slave-trade bills?
questions.audience: A history-minded reader sorting fact from common pro-life talking points
questions.answer: He calls the narrative scandalously false. Wilberforce in fact opposed the slave-carrying regulation bill and explicitly argued that gradualists - not slave traders themselves - became the most effective supporters of the slave trade by cooling parliamentary zeal with half-measures.
questions.quote: This is exactly opposite of the truth. If the truth is the North Pole, Alcorn and Challies are on the South Pole. If the truth is black, Alcorn and Challies are white. If the truth is hot, Alcorn and Challies are ice cold.

questions.question: How did Wilberforce himself describe the gradualists in Parliament?
questions.audience: A history-minded reader sorting fact from common pro-life talking points
questions.answer: Wilberforce wrote that the gradual abolitionists, by proposing gradual rather than immediate abolition, dashed the cup of happiness from the African's lips and effectively became the system's most efficient supporters - the real maintainers of the slave trade.
questions.quote: [L]et me most seriously urge on the conscientious deliberations of those who…by their fatal proposal of gradual, instead of immediate abolition, dashed the cup of happiness from the lips of the wretched African, at the very moment when at last he appeared likely to taste it, and who thus proved in fact the most efficient supporters of the Slave Trade.

questions.question: Who does Silberman say modern pro-life politicians actually resemble in the British abolition story?
questions.audience: A pastor weighing whether biblical fidelity or political pragmatism should drive strategy
questions.answer: Henry Dundas, not William Wilberforce. Dundas professed agreement with abolition's goal but used a procedural amendment to gut Wilberforce's bill into a gradualist measure - the same maneuver pro-life leaders use today to kill abolition bills while claiming pro-life credentials.
questions.quote: This is precisely the scheme pro-life politicians and lobbyists have concocted wherever an abolition bill has been introduced. The pro-lifers kill the abolition bill and pursue the incremental strategy with more gusto than they ever have before.

questions.question: Were the leading 19th-century American abolitionists also immediatists?
questions.audience: A history-minded reader sorting fact from common pro-life talking points
questions.answer: Yes - unanimously. The 1832 American Anti-Slavery Society declaration rejected any scheme as a substitute for immediate and total abolition, and figures like Garrison, Douglass, Stowe, Tubman, and the Tappan brothers all rejected incrementalism. Historians and reference works treat "immediatist" and "abolitionist" as synonyms.
questions.quote: The American Anti-Slavery Society's 1832 declaration of sentiments stated, "We regard as delusive, cruel and dangerous, any scheme of expatriation which pretends to aid, either directly or indirectly, in the emancipation of the slaves, or to be a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery."

questions.question: Why does Silberman call Alcorn's drowning-children analogy a category error?
questions.audience: A pro-lifer who considers abolitionism impractical in liberal states
questions.answer: Personal rescue - jumping into the water or going to the abortion mill - is inherently good. But legally regulating which preborn children may be killed is not analogous to saving the reachable child; it is doing evil that good may come. The analogy collapses two morally distinct categories.
questions.quote: Taking personal action to save children (jumping in the water, going to the abortion mill) is inherently good. Doing evil that good may come by legally regulating which preborn children can be drowned or murdered in the womb is not.

questions.question: What does Silberman believe will actually bring revival to a culture that loves child sacrifice?
questions.audience: A pastor weighing whether biblical fidelity or political pragmatism should drive strategy
questions.answer: Not telling people what they want to hear and not asking for compromised regulations. Revival comes through the Spirit moving through uncompromising proclamation of God's Word; making compromised demands actually stunts revival.
questions.quote: The Spirit moving through the uncompromising proclamation of the Word of God brings revival. Stop asking political questions and playing political games. Ask Biblical questions and come to Biblical solutions.

questions.question: What two questions does Silberman think will determine Alcorn's trajectory on abolitionism?
questions.audience: A Christian curious about the historical record on Wilberforce and slavery abolition
questions.answer: First, whether Alcorn will ask biblical questions and arrive at biblical solutions in the political realm. Second, whether he will be willing to risk his relationships with other pro-life leaders if he comes to see abolitionism as biblically faithful.
questions.quote:
1. Will he ask Biblical questions and come to Biblical solutions in the political context?
2. Is he willing to risk losing relationships with other pro-life leaders upon discovering the Biblical fidelity of abolitionism?

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Silberman links to this article himself as a foundational explanation of the immediatist position he defends against Alcorn.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Directly cited in the article on the dehumanizing effects of incremental laws and provides the systematic case against the bills Silberman critiques.

related.title: Watch: The Failure of Incremental Pro-Life Strategy
related.reason: Cited in-text as evidence for the claim that 49 years of incrementalism is a complete pragmatic failure.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Linked as a case study of the Henry Dundas pattern: a pro-life politician killing abolition bills while authoring incremental ones for cover.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Facade
related.reason: Linked example of Texas Rep. Jeff Leach using incremental legislation to obscure his role in blocking abolition - the modern Dundas scheme Silberman describes.

related.title: National Right to Life Endorses Greg McCortney, the Senator Who Kept Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Cited as evidence of pro-life organizations giving political cover to legislators who block abolition bills.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Parallel rebuttal to a popular Christian writer defending incrementalism on similar historical and pragmatic grounds.
